question: Procter & Gamble reported fiscal third-quarter 2026 net sales of $21.2 billion and 3% organic sales growth, but higher commodity costs were expected to pressure earnings.
Johnson & Johnson reported first-quarter 2026 sales of $24.1 billion and raised its full-year outlook after operational sales growth remained solid.
ServiceNow reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $3.77 billion, up 22% year over year, and raised its full-year subscription revenue outlook.
Booking Holdings grew 2025 gross bookings by 12% and revenue by 13%, although GAAP net income declined from the prior year.
Delta Air Lines generated $15.9 billion in March-quarter 2026 operating revenue and strong operating cash flow, but still reported a GAAP pre-tax loss.
RTX reported first-quarter 2026 sales of $22.1 billion, up 9% year over year, with adjusted EPS rising 21%.
Union Pacific reported higher first-quarter 2026 revenue and adjusted EPS, but warned that elevated fuel prices could pressure margins.
Home Depot's fiscal 2025 sales increased 3.2%, but net earnings and adjusted diluted EPS declined from the prior year.
Lockheed Martin's first-quarter 2026 earnings and operating cash flow fell from the prior year as production delays and contract costs weighed on results.
Nucor reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $9.5 billion and earnings of $3.23 per share as steel mills benefited from stronger prices and volumes.
$PG beat on sales and organic growth, but the commodity-cost warning makes the quarter feel more mixed than bullish.
$JNJ raising guidance after a solid Q1 sales print makes the healthcare giant look stronger than expected.
$NOW revenue up 22% and subscription guidance moving higher is a strong signal that enterprise AI demand is still real.
$BKNG travel demand still looks healthy, but lower GAAP net income keeps me from calling the year perfect.
$DAL pulled in strong revenue and cash flow, but a GAAP loss makes the quarter hard to read cleanly.
$RTX sales up 9% and adjusted EPS up 21% makes the aerospace and defense setup look pretty strong.
$UNP beat earnings, but fuel-price pressure and merger uncertainty keep the railroad story from being totally clean.
$HD sales grew for the year, but declining earnings show the home-improvement recovery is not fully back yet.
$LMT missing profit expectations while cash from operations drops is not a great look, even with defense demand high.
$NUE revenue jumping past expectations and steel mill shipments hitting records makes the quarter look very strong.
We delivered organic sales growth across all product categories, while higher commodity costs are expected to create near-term earnings pressure.
Reported sales increased to $24.1 billion, and we raised our full-year outlook based on continued operational momentum.
Subscription revenue grew 22% year over year, and we raised our full-year subscription revenue outlook.
Gross bookings and revenue increased at double-digit rates, although GAAP net income declined from the prior year.
Operating revenue and operating cash flow remained strong, but the quarter included a GAAP pre-tax loss.
Reported and adjusted sales increased 9%, and adjusted earnings per share rose 21% compared with the prior year.
Operating revenue grew 3% and adjusted EPS improved, while higher fuel prices remain a potential margin headwind.
Full-year sales increased, but net earnings and adjusted diluted EPS declined compared with the prior fiscal year.
Sales remained steady, but net earnings and cash from operations were lower than the prior-year quarter.
Steel mill performance improved significantly as higher average selling prices and record shipments supported a sharp earnings rebound.
General Motors said it was sourcing 100% renewable energy for its U.S. operations and matching 70% of global electricity use with renewables.
Fujifilm announced that its U.S. and Canadian operations would offset all electricity use through renewable energy certificates tied to a Texas solar project.
Fortescue committed an additional $680 million to green energy infrastructure as part of its plan to replace fossil fuels in Pilbara mining operations.
The U.S. Energy Department restored funding for major direct-air-capture carbon removal projects after previously considering cancellations.
Canada and Alberta moved closer to raising the carbon price for industrial emitters, but a broader oil-sands emissions agreement remained unresolved.
European rooftop-solar providers reported a jump in orders as households sought lower energy bills and greater energy independence.
Major technology companies signed an energy pledge aimed at improving efficiency and dedicated power supply as AI data-center demand strained electricity systems.
The Greenhouse Gas Protocol's incoming CEO emphasized consistent climate reporting while companies debated how Scope 2 and Scope 3 emissions should be measured.
EU countries approved changes that weaken corporate sustainability and supply-chain due-diligence rules for many companies.
A sustainability analysis warned that many companies still lack credible plans to deliver on their net-zero emissions promises.
Spain kept its 2026 economic growth forecast at 2.2% and projected improvements in public debt and budget deficit ratios.
French consumer confidence fell sharply in April 2026, dropping below expectations as households became more worried about finances and unemployment.
France's private-sector activity contracted at the fastest pace in 14 months as services weakness outweighed manufacturing improvement.
Italy's consumer and business confidence fell in April, and the government cut its 2026 and 2027 growth forecasts to 0.6%.
Vietnam's first-quarter 2026 GDP growth slowed to 7.83% from the previous quarter, although the economy still expanded at a strong pace.
Malaysia recorded 5.2% GDP growth in 2025, its fastest annual expansion in three years and above official projections.
Saudi Arabia's economy grew 4.5% in 2025, supported by non-oil activity, while firms still faced rising input and wage costs.
The UAE's decision to leave OPEC highlighted its economic diversification, with non-oil sectors accounting for more than three quarters of real GDP.
The Philippine central bank raised benchmark rates as Middle East conflict pushed energy and food inflation risks higher.
Egypt's growth outlook was trimmed after higher energy prices and regional conflict raised inflation and external-pressure risks.
